<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

content.text:
# Run Continuation - Resume Previous Work

Load a continuation prompt to pick up where a previous session left off.
Automatically detects worktree context and looks in the appropriate location.

## Usage

```bash
/run-continuation              # Show latest, confirm before loading
/run-continuation --yes        # Auto-load most recent (skip confirm)
/run-continuation --list       # Show all prompts, pick by number
/run-continuation --main       # Force main repo prompts (when in worktree)
/run-continuation api-feature  # Fuzzy match filename
```

## Execution

**This command delegates to `ccs prompts` for deterministic behavior.**

### 1. Get Prompts via CCS

| Flag | CCS Command | Result |
|------|-------------|--------|
| (default) | `ccs prompts --latest` | Get path to most recent prompt |
| `--list` | `ccs prompts --limit 10` | Show numbered list |
| `--main` | `ccs prompts --main --latest` | Force main repo |

CCS automatically:
- Detects worktree context (🌿 indicator)
- Checks `docs/prompts/worktrees/{name}/` for worktree-specific prompts
- Falls back to main `docs/prompts/` if no worktree prompts exist

### 2. Default Flow (no args)

1. Run `ccs prompts --latest` to get the most recent prompt path
2. Show the filename and ask with AskUserQuestion:
   - Header: "Load prompt"
   - Question: "Load this continuation prompt?"
   - Options: "Yes, load it" | "Show all prompts"

### 3. --yes Flag

Run `ccs prompts --latest`, read the file, display and follow it.

### 4. --list Flag

Run `ccs prompts --limit 10` to show recent prompts with status.
Use AskUserQuestion with numbered options for selection.

### 5. Fuzzy Match (filename arg)

Match the argument against output from `ccs prompts`.
If multiple matches, use AskUserQuestion to pick.

### 6. Load & Execute

Read the selected prompt file and display its full content:

```
✅ **Loading:** 2026-01-05-sop-system-continuation.md

---
[Full prompt content]
---
```

### 7. Auto-Create Tasks from Goals

After loading, parse the `## Goals` section and create a TaskCreate for each goal:
- Subject: goal title (from `### [ ] N. Title` lines)
- Description: first paragraph under each goal heading
- activeForm: present continuous of the goal title

Also create tasks for carry-over items if present.

Then follow the instructions in the prompt.

## Related

`/continuation-prompt` • `/handoff` • `ccs prompts --help`

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

9	title: cosmo-smoke v0.8.x — Post-OTel Continuation
10	---
11	
12	# cosmo-smoke v0.8.x — Post-OTel Continuation
13	
14	**Date**: 2026-04-19
15	**Branch**: master
16	**Status**: pending
17	
18	## Current State
19	
20	cosmo-smoke v0.8.0 with full OpenTelemetry integration. 314 tests passing, 29 assertion types. All 4 OTel depth roadmap items are **complete**:
21	
22	- ROAD-034: OpenTelemetry trace correlation (v0.8.0)
23	- ROAD-035: Export smoke results as OTel telemetry (OTLPReporter, MultiReporter)
24	- ROAD-036: Multi-backend trace verification (Jaeger/Tempo/Honeycomb/Datadog via traceBackend interface)
25	- ROAD-037: Trace-aware retry (retry_on_trace_only field on RetryPolicy)
26	- ROAD-038: Watch mode trace health monitoring (TraceHealthTracker sliding window)
27	
28	The OTel integration is feature-complete for v0.8.x. No pending OTel work.
29	
30	## Goal
31	
32	Pivot from OTel depth to strategic breadth. The next phase focuses on two directions:
33	
34	1. **Ecosystem expansion** — MCP extension for Claude Desktop integration (ROAD-032)
35	2. **Portfolio visibility** — Dashboard across the ~95-project portfolio (ROAD-039)
36	
37	Plus backlogged infrastructure items that unlock scale.
38	
39	## Strategic Priorities (Ordered)
40	
41	### Priority 1: ROAD-032 — Claude Desktop MCP Extension
42	
43	**Scope**: Large. Needs its own design session before implementation.
44	
45	The big strategic pivot. Makes cosmo-smoke usable from Claude Desktop via MCP protocol. Users can ask Claude to generate, run, and debug smoke tests conversationally.
46	
47	**What to figure out first**:
48	- MCP tool definitions: which smoke operations to expose (run, init, validate, list-tests)
49	- How to handle long-running test execution in MCP context (streaming vs polling)
50	- Config discovery: how Claude Desktop finds `.smoke.yaml` files in a project
51	- Error surfacing: assertion failures formatted for conversational debugging
52	- Whether this is a standalone MCP server binary or a mode of the existing `smoke` binary
53	
54	**Recommended approach**: Design session first (brainstorm + plan), then implement as `smoke mcp` subcommand or separate binary.
55	
56	### Priority 2: ROAD-039 — Portfolio Smoke Dashboard
57	
58	**Scope**: Medium-large.
59	
60	Dashboard that aggregates `smoke run --format json` results across the ~95-project CosmoLabs portfolio. Uses `smoke serve` endpoint to provide real-time status.
61	
62	**Key design questions**:
63	- Backend: in-process HTTP server in `smoke serve` or separate dashboard binary
64	- Data collection: pull model (dashboard fetches from each project) vs push (projects report to dashboard)
65	- Storage: in-memory ring buffer vs persistent store
66	- Auth: portfolio-wide API key or per-project tokens
67	
68	### Priority 3: ROAD-010 — Monorepo Sub-Config Support
69	
70	**Scope**: Medium. Has existing plan.
71	
72	`--monorepo` flag auto-discovers `.smoke.yaml` in subdirectories. Unlimited depth, configurable exclusions. Already has architectural design in place.
73	
74	**Carry-over**: Check `docs/planning-mode/` for existing plan file.
75	
76	### Priority 4: ROAD-033 — Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP
77	
78	**Scope**: Small. Process improvement.
79	
80	SOP for handling merge conflicts when multiple GLM agents modify overlapping files. Documentation-only.
81	
82	## Open Ideas
83	
84	- **IDEA-MO1FC22M**: Mobile app deep link assertion — seed status, recently updated with multi-project-type scope (Android/iOS/React Native). Worth exploring if mobile testing becomes a portfolio need.
85	
86	## Carry-Overs
87	
88	### Technical Context
89	- 314 tests, all passing. Test suite is healthy.
90	- Build: `go build ./...` — clean. Binary at `./smoke`.
91	- OTel code lives in `internal/runner/` (reporter, trace backend, retry integration).
92	- Reporter interface in `internal/reporter/` supports pluggable formats.
93	- Config schema in `internal/schema/` — all assertion types are pure functions.
94	
95	### Architecture Notes for MCP (ROAD-032)
96	- `cmd/` uses Cobra — new subcommand follows established pattern
97	- Reporter interface already abstracts output format — MCP can add a `MCPReporter`
98	- `internal/schema/` has YAML parsing + validation that MCP tools can reuse
99	- Existing `smoke init` already does project detection + template generation — MCP tool can call same logic
100	- No external HTTP server dependency currently — `smoke serve` for dashboard would be new territory
101	
102	## Session Entry Recommendations
103	
104	1. Start with `/triage` to get full work source scan
105	2. For ROAD-032: run `/brainplan "Claude Desktop MCP extension for cosmo-smoke"` — this needs design before code
106	3. For ROAD-039: check if any brainstorming docs already exist in `docs/ideas/`
107	4. Commit any uncommitted changes from this session before starting new work
108	5. Consider bumping version to v0.9.0-dev after committing the OTel changelog entries
109	
110	## Reference Files
111	
112	- `CLAUDE.md` — project architecture, assertion types, build/test commands
113	- `docs/roadmap/index.yaml` — full roadmap with status
114	- `docs/ideas/2026-04-16-portfolio-smoke-dashboard.md` — dashboard idea
115	- `docs/ideas/2026-04-16-graphql-introspection-assertion.md` — recently implemented
116	- `internal/runner/` — assertion engine, OTel reporter, trace backends
117	- `internal/reporter/` — terminal/JSON/JUnit/TAP/Prometheus reporters
118	- `internal/schema/` — config structs and validation
119	
120	## What Got Done (Amendment — 2026-04-19)
121	
122	**Session type**: Cleanup and session-end. No new feature work started.
123	
124	**Commits made**: 5 commits on master, all uncommitted changes from previous session were committed:
125	- `b2bef56` chore: session-end documentation
126	- `fd6d4ab` chore: update GOrchestra intel metadata
127	- `7bb8e58` docs: session-end documentation and metadata updates
128	- `4ba01d8` docs: add USAGE.md to all 11 CCS-managed directories
129	- `0e8f984` chore: upgrade project to CCS standards
130	
131	**Working tree**: Clean after commits. No uncommitted changes remaining.
132	
133	**Strategic priorities**: Unchanged. None of these were touched:
134	- ROAD-032 (MCP extension) — still priority 1, still needs design session
135	- ROAD-039 (Portfolio dashboard) — still priority 2
136	- ROAD-010 (Monorepo sub-config) — still priority 3
137	
138	**Version**: Still v0.8.0. No version bump performed. The changelog entries for OTel features remain in `docs/changelog/unreleased.yaml` — these should be released before bumping to v0.9.0-dev.
139	
140	**Status**: Still `pending` — no strategic roadmap work was completed this session.
141	
142	## What Got Done (Amendment 2 — 2026-04-19)
143	
144	**Session type**: Heavy implementation. Major feature work across 4 roadmap items.
145	
146	### ROAD-010: Monorepo Sub-Config Support — DONE (already implemented)
147	
148	Verified that monorepo sub-config support was already implemented in v0.7. Updated roadmap status to completed. No code changes needed.
149	
150	### ROAD-033: Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP — DONE
151	
152	Wrote and committed the merge-conflict SOP at `docs/instructions/merge-conflict-sop.md`. Process improvement, documentation-only deliverable.
153	
154	### ROAD-032: Claude Desktop MCP Extension — Design Complete
155	
156	Brainstormed the full MCP extension design in `docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-claude-desktop-mcp-extension.md`. Key decisions:

title: cosmo-smoke v0.8.x — Post-OTel Continuation
---

# cosmo-smoke v0.8.x — Post-OTel Continuation

**Date**: 2026-04-19
**Branch**: master
**Status**: pending

## Current State

cosmo-smoke v0.8.0 with full OpenTelemetry integration. 314 tests passing, 29 assertion types. All 4 OTel depth roadmap items are **complete**:

- ROAD-034: OpenTelemetry trace correlation (v0.8.0)
- ROAD-035: Export smoke results as OTel telemetry (OTLPReporter, MultiReporter)
- ROAD-036: Multi-backend trace verification (Jaeger/Tempo/Honeycomb/Datadog via traceBackend interface)
- ROAD-037: Trace-aware retry (retry_on_trace_only field on RetryPolicy)
- ROAD-038: Watch mode trace health monitoring (TraceHealthTracker sliding window)

The OTel integration is feature-complete for v0.8.x. No pending OTel work.

## Goal

Pivot from OTel depth to strategic breadth. The next phase focuses on two directions:

1. **Ecosystem expansion** — MCP extension for Claude Desktop integration (ROAD-032)
2. **Portfolio visibility** — Dashboard across the ~95-project portfolio (ROAD-039)

Plus backlogged infrastructure items that unlock scale.

## Strategic Priorities (Ordered)

### Priority 1: ROAD-032 — Claude Desktop MCP Extension

**Scope**: Large. Needs its own design session before implementation.

The big strategic pivot. Makes cosmo-smoke usable from Claude Desktop via MCP protocol. Users can ask Claude to generate, run, and debug smoke tests conversationally.

**What to figure out first**:
- MCP tool definitions: which smoke operations to expose (run, init, validate, list-tests)
- How to handle long-running test execution in MCP context (streaming vs polling)
- Config discovery: how Claude Desktop finds `.smoke.yaml` files in a project
- Error surfacing: assertion failures formatted for conversational debugging
- Whether this is a standalone MCP server binary or a mode of the existing `smoke` binary

**Recommended approach**: Design session first (brainstorm + plan), then implement as `smoke mcp` subcommand or separate binary.

### Priority 2: ROAD-039 — Portfolio Smoke Dashboard

**Scope**: Medium-large.

Dashboard that aggregates `smoke run --format json` results across the ~95-project CosmoLabs portfolio. Uses `smoke serve` endpoint to provide real-time status.

**Key design questions**:
- Backend: in-process HTTP server in `smoke serve` or separate dashboard binary
- Data collection: pull model (dashboard fetches from each project) vs push (projects report to dashboard)
- Storage: in-memory ring buffer vs persistent store
- Auth: portfolio-wide API key or per-project tokens

### Priority 3: ROAD-010 — Monorepo Sub-Config Support

**Scope**: Medium. Has existing plan.

`--monorepo` flag auto-discovers `.smoke.yaml` in subdirectories. Unlimited depth, configurable exclusions. Already has architectural design in place.

**Carry-over**: Check `docs/planning-mode/` for existing plan file.

### Priority 4: ROAD-033 — Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP

**Scope**: Small. Process improvement.

SOP for handling merge conflicts when multiple GLM agents modify overlapping files. Documentation-only.

## Open Ideas

- **IDEA-MO1FC22M**: Mobile app deep link assertion — seed status, recently updated with multi-project-type scope (Android/iOS/React Native). Worth exploring if mobile testing becomes a portfolio need.

## Carry-Overs

### Technical Context
- 314 tests, all passing. Test suite is healthy.
- Build: `go build ./...` — clean. Binary at `./smoke`.
- OTel code lives in `internal/runner/` (reporter, trace backend, retry integration).
- Reporter interface in `internal/reporter/` supports pluggable formats.
- Config schema in `internal/schema/` — all assertion types are pure functions.

### Architecture Notes for MCP (ROAD-032)
- `cmd/` uses Cobra — new subcommand follows established pattern
- Reporter interface already abstracts output format — MCP can add a `MCPReporter`
- `internal/schema/` has YAML parsing + validation that MCP tools can reuse
- Existing `smoke init` already does project detection + template generation — MCP tool can call same logic
- No external HTTP server dependency currently — `smoke serve` for dashboard would be new territory

## Session Entry Recommendations

1. Start with `/triage` to get full work source scan
2. For ROAD-032: run `/brainplan "Claude Desktop MCP extension for cosmo-smoke"` — this needs design before code
3. For ROAD-039: check if any brainstorming docs already exist in `docs/ideas/`
4. Commit any uncommitted changes from this session before starting new work
5. Consider bumping version to v0.9.0-dev after committing the OTel changelog entries

## Reference Files

- `CLAUDE.md` — project architecture, assertion types, build/test commands
- `docs/roadmap/index.yaml` — full roadmap with status
- `docs/ideas/2026-04-16-portfolio-smoke-dashboard.md` — dashboard idea
- `docs/ideas/2026-04-16-graphql-introspection-assertion.md` — recently implemented
- `internal/runner/` — assertion engine, OTel reporter, trace backends
- `internal/reporter/` — terminal/JSON/JUnit/TAP/Prometheus reporters
- `internal/schema/` — config structs and validation

## What Got Done (Amendment — 2026-04-19)

**Session type**: Cleanup and session-end. No new feature work started.

**Commits made**: 5 commits on master, all uncommitted changes from previous session were committed:
- `b2bef56` chore: session-end documentation
- `fd6d4ab` chore: update GOrchestra intel metadata
- `7bb8e58` docs: session-end documentation and metadata updates
- `4ba01d8` docs: add USAGE.md to all 11 CCS-managed directories
- `0e8f984` chore: upgrade project to CCS standards

**Working tree**: Clean after commits. No uncommitted changes remaining.

**Strategic priorities**: Unchanged. None of these were touched:
- ROAD-032 (MCP extension) — still priority 1, still needs design session
- ROAD-039 (Portfolio dashboard) — still priority 2
- ROAD-010 (Monorepo sub-config) — still priority 3

**Version**: Still v0.8.0. No version bump performed. The changelog entries for OTel features remain in `docs/changelog/unreleased.yaml` — these should be released before bumping to v0.9.0-dev.

**Status**: Still `pending` — no strategic roadmap work was completed this session.

## What Got Done (Amendment 2 — 2026-04-19)

**Session type**: Heavy implementation. Major feature work across 4 roadmap items.

### ROAD-010: Monorepo Sub-Config Support — DONE (already implemented)

Verified that monorepo sub-config support was already implemented in v0.7. Updated roadmap status to completed. No code changes needed.

### ROAD-033: Parallel-Agent Merge-Conflict SOP — DONE

Wrote and committed the merge-conflict SOP at `docs/instructions/merge-conflict-sop.md`. Process improvement, documentation-only deliverable.

### ROAD-032: Claude Desktop MCP Extension — Design Complete

Brainstormed the full MCP extension design in `docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-claude-desktop-mcp-extension.md`. Key decisions:

- 7 MCP tools: `run_smoke`, `init_config`, `validate_config`, `list_tests`, `get_last_results`, `debug_failure`, `explain_assertion`
- Implementation as `smoke mcp` subcommand using stdio transport
- Library: `mark3labs/mcp-go` for Go MCP server implementation
- Estimated ~1350 LOC across 5 new files
- Ready for implementation in a future session

### ROAD-039: Portfolio Smoke Dashboard — DONE (Fully Implemented)

All 5 phases completed this session:

1. **PushReporter** — JSON push to configurable endpoint
2. **SQLite storage** — Persistent result storage with `internal/dashboard/store.go`
3. **API handlers** — REST API (`/api/results`, `/api/summary`, `/api/projects`, `/api/runs`) in `internal/dashboard/api.go`
4. **Embedded HTML frontend** — Single-file dashboard UI in `internal/dashboard/frontend.html`
5. **Documentation** — Full feature docs

**13 new tests** written and passing. Total test count now **327 tests passing**.

### Version Bump

Bumped version from v0.8.0 to **v0.9.0**.

### Feedback Sent

- **FB-565**: Roadmap YAML duplicate key handling — minor tooling improvement suggestion

### Updated Strategic Priorities

| Item | Status |
|------|--------|
| ROAD-032: MCP extension | Ready for implementation (design complete) |
| ROAD-039: Portfolio dashboard | **DONE** — implemented this session |
| ROAD-033: Merge-conflict SOP | **DONE** — written this session |
| ROAD-010: Monorepo sub-config | **DONE** — verified already in v0.7 |

**Next session should focus on**: ROAD-032 implementation (MCP extension). Design doc is ready in `docs/brainstorming/2026-04-19-claude-desktop-mcp-extension.md`.

content.content:
1	# Claude Desktop MCP Extension for cosmo-smoke
2	
3	**Date**: 2026-04-19
4	**Roadmap**: ROAD-032
5	**Status**: Brainstorm
6	
7	## Goal
8	
9	Make cosmo-smoke usable from Claude Desktop (and any MCP client) via the Model Context Protocol. Users ask Claude to generate, run, and debug smoke tests conversationally -- no CLI knowledge required.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. MCP Tool Definitions
14	
15	Seven tools covering the full smoke test lifecycle. Each maps to existing internal packages with minimal new logic.
16	
17	### 1.1 `smoke_run` -- Execute smoke tests
18	
19	**Purpose**: Run tests and return structured results for conversational debugging.
20	
21	```json
22	{
23	  "name": "smoke_run",
24	  "description": "Run smoke tests from a .smoke.yaml config file. Returns pass/fail results with assertion details for each test. Use this to verify services are healthy, check endpoints, validate configs, or debug failures.",
25	  "inputSchema": {
26	    "type": "object",
27	    "properties": {
28	      "config_path": {
29	        "type": "string",
30	        "description": "Path to .smoke.yaml (default: .smoke.yaml in working directory)"
31	      },
32	      "tags": {
33	        "type": "array",
34	        "items": { "type": "string" },
35	        "description": "Include only tests with these tags"
36	      },
37	      "exclude_tags": {
38	        "type": "array",
39	        "items": { "type": "string" },
40	        "description": "Exclude tests with these tags"
41	      },
42	      "fail_fast": {
43	        "type": "boolean",
44	        "description": "Stop on first failure (default: false)"
45	      },
46	      "timeout": {
47	        "type": "string",
48	        "description": "Per-test timeout override, e.g. '30s'"
49	      },
50	      "dry_run": {
51	        "type": "boolean",
52	        "description": "List tests without running them (default: false)"
53	      },
54	      "monorepo": {
55	        "type": "boolean",
56	        "description": "Discover and run .smoke.yaml in subdirectories (default: false)"
57	      },
58	      "env": {
59	        "type": "string",
60	        "description": "Load environment-specific config (e.g. 'staging' loads staging.smoke.yaml)"
61	      }
62	    }
63	  },
64	  "annotations": {
65	    "title": "Run Smoke Tests",
66	    "readOnlyHint": false,
67	    "destructiveHint": false,
68	    "idempotentHint": false,
69	    "openWorldHint": true
70	  }
71	}
72	```
73	
74	**Returns**: JSON-structured result reusing `reporter/json.go` format (project, total, passed, failed, per-test assertion details with expected vs actual). On failure, includes `fix_suggestions` (see Section 5).
75	
76	**Reuses**: `runner.Runner.Run()`, `runner.Runner.RunMonorepo()`, `schema.Load()`, `schema.MergeEnv()`, `reporter.JSON` pattern.
77	
78	### 1.2 `smoke_init` -- Generate config for a project
79	
80	**Purpose**: Auto-detect project type and generate `.smoke.yaml`. Conversational onboarding.
81	
82	```json
83	{
84	  "name": "smoke_init",
85	  "description": "Generate a .smoke.yaml smoke test config for a project. Auto-detects Go, Node, Python, Docker, and Rust projects. Can also inspect a running Docker container. Returns the generated config without writing to disk unless confirm=true.",
86	  "inputSchema": {
87	    "type": "object",
88	    "properties": {
89	      "directory": {
90	        "type": "string",
91	        "description": "Project directory to scan (default: working directory)"
92	      },
93	      "from_container": {
94	        "type": "string",
95	        "description": "Generate config by inspecting a running Docker container name"
96	      },
97	      "write": {
98	        "type": "boolean",
99	        "description": "Write .smoke.yaml to disk (default: false, returns YAML as text)"
100	      },
101	      "force": {
102	        "type": "boolean",
103	        "description": "Overwrite existing .smoke.yaml (default: false)"
104	      }
105	    }
106	  },
107	  "annotations": {
108	    "title": "Generate Smoke Test Config",
109	    "readOnlyHint": false,
110	    "destructiveHint": false,
111	    "idempotentHint": false,
112	    "openWorldHint": false
113	  }
114	}
115	```
116	
117	**Returns**: Generated YAML as text. If `write=true`, confirms file path written.
118	
119	**Reuses**: `detector.Detect()`, `detector.GenerateConfig()`, `detector.InspectContainer()`.
120	
121	### 1.3 `smoke_validate` -- Validate a config file
122	
123	**Purpose**: Check `.smoke.yaml` for errors without running tests. Returns all validation errors at once.
124	
125	```json
126	{
127	  "name": "smoke_validate",
128	  "description": "Validate a .smoke.yaml config file without running tests. Checks for required fields, assertion consistency, regex validity, and structural correctness. Returns all errors at once.",
129	  "inputSchema": {
130	    "type": "object",
131	    "properties": {
132	      "config_path": {
133	        "type": "string",
134	        "description": "Path to .smoke.yaml (default: .smoke.yaml)"
135	      }
136	    }
137	  },
138	  "annotations": {
139	    "title": "Validate Smoke Config",
140	    "readOnlyHint": true,
141	    "destructiveHint": false,
142	    "idempotentHint": true,
143	    "openWorldHint": false
144	  }
145	}
146	```
147	
148	**Returns**: Valid (list of tests found) or list of validation errors with field paths.
149	
150	**Reuses**: `schema.Load()`, `schema.Validate()`.
151	
152	### 1.4 `smoke_list` -- List tests in a config
153	
154	**Purpose**: Enumerate available tests, their assertions, and tags. Lets Claude understand what's configured before running.
155	
156	```json
157	{
158	  "name": "smoke_list",
159	  "description": "List all smoke tests defined in a .smoke.yaml config. Shows test names, tags, command, and assertion types. Useful for understanding what's configured before running.",
160	  "inputSchema": {
161	    "type": "object",
162	    "properties": {
163	      "config_path": {
164	        "type": "string",
165	        "description": "Path to .smoke.yaml (default: .smoke.yaml)"
166	      },
167	      "tags": {
168	        "type": "array",
169	        "items": { "type": "string" },
170	        "description": "Filter to tests with these tags"
171	      },
172	      "monorepo": {
173	        "type": "boolean",
174	        "description": "Discover configs in subdirectories (default: false)"
175	      }
176	    }
177	  },
178	  "annotations": {
179	    "title": "List Smoke Tests",
180	    "readOnlyHint": true,
181	    "destructiveHint": false,
182	    "idempotentHint": true,
183	    "openWorldHint": false
184	  }
185	}
186	```
187	
188	**Returns**: Array of `{name, tags, run_command, assertion_types[], skip_if}`.
189	
190	**Reuses**: `schema.Load()`, `monorepo.Discover()`.
191	
192	### 1.5 `smoke_explain` -- Explain an assertion type
193	
194	**Purpose**: Claude can look up what an assertion does and how to configure it. Reduces hallucination about assertion fields.
195	
196	```json
197	{
198	  "name": "smoke_explain",
199	  "description": "Explain a smoke test assertion type and its configuration. Returns the assertion's fields, defaults, and an example YAML snippet. Use when you need to understand or construct assertion configurations.",
200	  "inputSchema": {
201	    "type": "object",
202	    "properties": {
203	      "assertion_type": {
204	        "type": "string",
205	        "description": "Assertion type to explain",
206	        "enum": [
207	          "exit_code", "stdout_contains", "stdout_matches",
208	          "stderr_contains", "stderr_matches", "file_exists",
209	          "env_exists", "port_listening", "process_running",
210	          "http", "json_field", "response_time_ms",
211	          "ssl_cert", "redis_ping", "memcached_version",
212	          "postgres_ping", "mysql_ping", "grpc_health",
213	          "websocket", "docker_container_running",
214	          "docker_image_exists", "url_reachable",
215	          "service_reachable", "s3_bucket", "version_check",
216	          "otel_trace", "credential_check", "graphql"
217	        ]
218	      }
219	    },
220	    "required": ["assertion_type"]
221	  },
222	  "annotations": {
223	    "title": "Explain Assertion Type",
224	    "readOnlyHint": true,
225	    "destructiveHint": false,
226	    "idempotentHint": true,
227	    "openWorldHint": false
228	  }
229	}
230	```
231	
232	**Returns**: Field descriptions, defaults, YAML example, notes on standalone vs command-based usage.
233	
234	**Implementation**: Static lookup table in `internal/mcp/assertions.go`. No runtime dependency on existing packages -- pure data.
235	
236	### 1.6 `smoke_discover` -- Find configs in a directory tree
237	
238	**Purpose**: Locate `.smoke.yaml` files across a workspace or monorepo.
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "name": "smoke_discover",
243	  "description": "Find all .smoke.yaml config files in a directory tree. Returns paths and project names. Useful for understanding the test landscape of a workspace.",
244	  "inputSchema": {
245	    "type": "object",
246	    "properties": {
247	      "directory": {
248	        "type": "string",
249	        "description": "Root directory to search (default: working directory)"
250	      },
251	      "depth": {
252	        "type": "number",
253	        "description": "Maximum search depth (default: unlimited)"
254	      }
255	    }
256	  },
257	  "annotations": {
258	    "title": "Discover Smoke Configs",
259	    "readOnlyHint": true,
260	    "destructiveHint": false,
261	    "idempotentHint": true,
262	    "openWorldHint": false
263	  }
264	}
265	```
266	
267	**Returns**: Array of `{path, directory, project_name}`.
268	
269	**Reuses**: `monorepo.Discover()`.
270	
271	### 1.7 `smoke_generate_test` -- Generate a single test
272	
273	**Purpose**: Generate a YAML snippet for a specific test case. Claude describes what to test, this returns valid YAML.
274	
275	```json
276	{
277	  "name": "smoke_generate_test",
278	  "description": "Generate a single smoke test YAML snippet. Provide what you want to test and get back valid YAML to add to .smoke.yaml. Supports all 29 assertion types.",
279	  "inputSchema": {
280	    "type": "object",
281	    "properties": {
282	      "name": {
283	        "type": "string",
284	        "description": "Test name"
285	      },
286	      "description": {
287	        "type": "string",
288	        "description": "What this test should verify (natural language)"
289	      },
290	      "assertion_type": {
291	        "type": "string",
292	        "description": "Primary assertion type (e.g. 'http', 'port_listening', 'redis_ping')"
293	      },
294	      "params": {
295	        "type": "object",
296	        "description": "Assertion parameters as key-value pairs",
297	        "additionalProperties": true
298	      },
299	      "tags": {
300	        "type": "array",
301	        "items": { "type": "string" },
302	        "description": "Tags for this test"
303	      }
304	    },
305	    "required": ["name", "assertion_type"]
306	  },
307	  "annotations": {
308	    "title": "Generate Smoke Test",
309	    "readOnlyHint": true,
310	    "destructiveHint": false,
311	    "idempotentHint": true,
312	    "openWorldHint": false
313	  }
314	}
315	```
316	
317	**Returns**: Valid YAML snippet for the test.
318	
319	**Implementation**: Template-based generation using `internal/detector/templates.go` patterns.
320	
321	---
322	
323	## 2. Architecture Decision: `smoke mcp` Subcommand vs Standalone Binary
324	
325	### Option A: `smoke mcp` subcommand
326	
327	Add a `cmd/mcp.go` that starts the MCP server via stdio.
328	
329	**Pros**:
330	- Single binary distribution -- no new build target
331	- Shares version, root command banner, ldflags injection
332	- Users already have `smoke` installed; just run `smoke mcp`
333	- Claude Desktop config: `"command": "smoke", "args": ["mcp"]`
334	
335	**Cons**:
336	- Binary size grows with `mcp-go` dependency (~200KB)
337	- Users who don't use MCP still pull in the dependency
338	- Longer process startup (Cobra init, though negligible)
339	
340	### Option B: Standalone `smoke-mcp` binary
341	
342	Separate `cmd/smoke-mcp/main.go` with its own build target.
343	
344	**Pros**:
345	- Clean separation -- MCP users get a focused binary
346	- No dependency bloat for core `smoke` users
347	- Can version independently
348	
349	**Cons**:
350	- Two binaries to build, distribute, and version
351	- Duplicate schema/runner/reporter imports
352	- More complex Makefile, release process, and Homebrew formula
353	- Users must install two tools
354	
355	### Recommendation: Option A -- `smoke mcp` subcommand
356	
357	Justification:
358	1. cosmo-smoke's philosophy is "minimal deps" but mcp-go (~200KB compiled) is modest.
359	2. Single-binary UX is critical for Claude Desktop adoption. Users install `smoke` once, add `"command": "smoke", "args": ["mcp"]` to their MCP config, done.
360	3. The existing `smoke serve` command already established the pattern of long-running subcommands.
361	4. Can use a build tag (`//go:build mcp`) to make it opt-in if dependency size becomes a concern later.
362	5. The Go plugin landscape for MCP is nascent -- having one binary avoids version skew between `smoke` and `smoke-mcp` as the protocol evolves.
363	
364	**Claude Desktop configuration** (what the user adds):
365	```json
366	{
367	  "mcpServers": {
368	    "cosmo-smoke": {
369	      "command": "smoke",
370	      "args": ["mcp"]
371	    }
372	  }
373	}
374	```
375	
376	---
377	
378	## 3. Long-Running Tests
379	
380	### Problem
381	
382	Smoke tests can take 30s+ (HTTP timeouts, otel_trace waits, retry with backoff). MCP tool calls should return within a reasonable time. Claude Desktop shows no progress UI during tool execution.
383	
384	### Design: Task-Augmented Tools via mcp-go
385	
386	mcp-go v0.34+ supports **task-augmented tools** with three modes:
387	- `TaskSupportForbidden` -- synchronous only (default)
388	- `TaskSupportOptional` -- can run sync or async
389	- `TaskSupportRequired` -- always async, returns task ID for polling
390	
391	**Recommendation**: Use `TaskSupportOptional` for `smoke_run`.
392	
393	**Flow**:
394	
395	1. **Fast tests (<30s)**: Claude calls `smoke_run` without task parameter. Synchronous response. Most smoke tests complete in 1-10s.
396	
397	2. **Slow tests (>30s)**: Claude calls `smoke_run` with `{ "_task": {} }` parameter. Server returns a task ID immediately. Claude polls `tasks/result` to get the outcome.
398	
399	3. **Progress notifications**: While the task runs, the server sends `notifications/tasks/{taskId}` with status updates (running, percentage complete based on tests completed / total tests).
400	
401	**Implementation**:
402	
403	```go
404	// cmd/mcp.go
405	smokeRunTool := mcp.NewTool("smoke_run",
406	    mcp.WithDescription("Run smoke tests..."),
407	    mcp.WithTaskSupport(mcp.TaskSupportOptional),
408	    // ... other params
409	)
410	
411	s.AddTaskTool(smokeRunTool, func(ctx context.Context, req mcp.CallToolRequest) (*mcp.CreateTaskResult, error) {
412	    // Execute tests in background
413	    // Send progress notifications via ctx
414	    // Return task result
415	})
416	```
417	
418	**Timeout handling**:
419	- Default per-test timeout: 30s (matches existing behavior in `runner.go:296`)
420	- Total suite timeout: sum of all test timeouts + 60s buffer
421	- Context cancellation from MCP client propagates to `exec.CommandContext` in runner
422	
423	**What about other tools?**: `smoke_validate`, `smoke_list`, `smoke_explain`, `smoke_discover`, `smoke_generate_test` are all fast (<1s) and always synchronous. `smoke_init` is fast for auto-detect but `from_container` inspection could take 5-10s -- still fine for synchronous.
424	
425	---
426	
427	## 4. Config Discovery
428	
429	### Working Directory
430	
431	MCP servers launched by Claude Desktop inherit the working directory from the Claude Desktop process, which is typically `$HOME`. This is wrong for project-scoped smoke tests.
432	
433	**Solution**: All tools that take a `config_path` or `directory` parameter resolve relative to the **project root**, not the process cwd. Detection strategy:
434	
435	1. **Explicit path**: If user says "run smoke tests in /Users/gab/myproject", pass `config_path=/Users/gab/myproject/.smoke.yaml`.
436	2. **Claude Desktop workspace**: Claude Desktop passes project context to tools. The MCP handler checks if the `config_path` is relative and resolves it against the workspace root if available.
437	3. **Walk up**: If no path specified, walk up from cwd looking for `.smoke.yaml`, `.git/`, or `go.mod` to find the project root. This matches how `smoke run` already works (defaults to `.smoke.yaml` in cwd).
438	
439	**Implementation in handler**:
440	
441	```go
442	func resolveConfigPath(configPath string) (string, error) {
443	    if configPath == "" {
444	        configPath = ".smoke.yaml"
445	    }
446	    if filepath.IsAbs(configPath) {
447	        return configPath, nil
448	    }
449	    // Walk up from cwd looking for project root markers
450	    cwd, _ := os.Getwd()
451	    dir := cwd
452	    for {
453	        if _, err := os.Stat(filepath.Join(dir, configPath)); err == nil {
454	            return filepath.Join(dir, configPath), nil
455	        }
456	        parent := filepath.Dir(dir)
457	        if parent == dir {
458	            break
459	        }
460	        dir = parent
461	    }
462	    return "", fmt.Errorf("no %s found in %s or any parent", configPath, cwd)
463	}
464	```
465	
466	### Monorepo Support
467	
468	The `smoke_run` and `smoke_list` tools accept a `monorepo: true` parameter, which triggers `monorepo.Discover()` exactly as the CLI does today. The MCP handler reuses `runner.Runner.RunMonorepo()`.
469	
470	For `smoke_discover`, the tool walks a directory tree finding all `.smoke.yaml` files. This is a lightweight wrapper around `monorepo.Discover()` with an optional depth parameter.
471	
472	### Multi-Project Sessions
473	
474	Claude Desktop may have multiple projects open. Each `smoke_run` call specifies which project via `config_path`. No session-level state is needed -- each tool call is self-contained. This is stateless by design and avoids the complexity of per-session working directory tracking.
475	
476	---
477	
478	## 5. Error Surfacing for Conversational Debugging
479	
480	### Design Principle
481	
482	When a test fails, Claude needs enough context to explain *why* and suggest *what to do*. Raw assertion output is not enough -- we need conversational remediation hints.
483	
484	### Error Response Format
485	
486	Every `smoke_run` result includes a structured JSON body:
487	
488	```json
489	{
490	  "tool_result": {
491	    "status": "failed",
492	    "summary": {
493	      "total": 6,
494	      "passed": 4,
495	      "failed": 1,
496	      "skipped": 1,
497	      "duration_ms": 3200
498	    },
499	    "tests": [
500	      {
501	        "name": "API health check",
502	        "status": "passed",
503	        "duration_ms": 150
504	      },
505	      {
506	        "name": "Redis connectivity",
507	        "status": "failed",
508	        "duration_ms": 50,
509	        "assertions": [
510	          {
511	            "type": "redis_ping",
512	            "expected": "+PONG response from Redis",
513	            "actual": "connection refused on localhost:6379",
514	            "passed": false
515	          }
516	        ],
517	        "fix_suggestions": [
518	          "Redis is not running or not listening on localhost:6379",
519	          "Start Redis: docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:alpine",
520	          "If Redis is on a different host/port, update redis_ping.host and redis_ping.port in .smoke.yaml"
521	        ]
522	      }
523	    ]
524	  }
525	}
526	```
527	
528	### Fix Suggestion Engine
529	
530	Each assertion type has a suggestion mapper. This is a static lookup keyed by assertion type + failure pattern.
531	
532	```go
533	// internal/mcp/suggestions.go
534	
535	type Suggestion struct {
536	    Condition string // "connection_refused", "timeout", "wrong_status", "not_found", etc.
537	    Message   string
538	    Action    string // concrete fix command or config change
539	}
540	
541	var suggestionMap = map[string][]Suggestion{
542	    "redis_ping": {
543	        {Condition: "connection_refused", Message: "Redis is not running", Action: "docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:alpine"},
544	        {Condition: "auth_failed", Message: "Redis requires authentication", Action: "Add redis_ping.password to .smoke.yaml"},
545	    },
546	    "http": {
547	        {Condition: "connection_refused", Message: "Server not listening", Action: "Check if the service is running and the port is correct"},
548	        {Condition: "wrong_status", Message: "Unexpected HTTP status code", Action: "Verify the endpoint returns the expected status code"},
549	        {Condition: "timeout", Message: "Request timed out", Action: "Increase timeout or check if the service is overloaded"},
550	    },
551	    "port_listening": {
552	        {Condition: "not_listening", Message: "Port is not open", Action: "Start the service that should listen on this port"},
553	    },
554	    "postgres_ping": {
555	        {Condition: "connection_refused", Message: "Postgres not running", Action: "docker run -d -p 5432:5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres postgres:alpine"},
556	    },
557	    // ... for all 29 assertion types
558	}
559	```
560	
561	The suggestion engine extracts the failure condition from `AssertionResult.Actual` and matches it against known patterns. Fallback: generic "check the configuration for this assertion type" message.
562	
563	### Truncation for Large Output
564	
565	If stdout/stderr in assertion actual values exceeds 2KB, truncate with `[... truncated, full output: X bytes]`. This prevents context window flooding. Claude can ask the user to run a targeted test if they need the full output.
566	
567	---
568	
569	## 6. Dependencies
570	
571	### Primary: `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go`
572	
573	- **Current version**: v0.34.0 (latest as of 2026-04)
574	- **MCP spec version**: 2025-11-25 (backward compat to 2024-11-05)
575	- **Transport**: stdio (for Claude Desktop)
576	- **Features needed**: Tools, Task-augmented tools, Progress notifications
577	- **License**: MIT
578	- **Stars**: ~4k, actively maintained
579	
580	**Usage pattern**:
581	```go
582	import (
583	    "github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go/mcp"
584	    "github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go/server"
585	)
586	```
587	
588	**Key API surface we need**:
589	- `server.NewMCPServer()` -- server creation
590	- `mcp.NewTool()` -- tool definition with typed params
591	- `s.AddTool()` -- sync tool handler
592	- `s.AddTaskTool()` -- async tool handler (for `smoke_run`)
593	- `server.ServeStdio()` -- stdio transport for Claude Desktop
594	- `server.WithToolCapabilities()` -- enable tool listing
595	- `server.WithRecovery()` -- panic recovery in handlers
596	- `server.WithHooks()` -- request lifecycle hooks for logging
597	
598	### No new runtime dependencies
599	
600	mcp-go is the only new dependency. It has its own transitive deps but they don't conflict with cosmo-smoke's existing deps (Cobra, Lipgloss, yaml.v3, gjson, fsnotify, grpc).
601	
602	### Dependency audit
603	
604	| Package | Added | Purpose |
605	|---------|-------|---------|
606	| `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go` | Yes | MCP protocol implementation |
607	| `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go/mcp` | Yes (transitive) | Tool/resource types |
608	| `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go/server` | Yes (transitive) | Server + transport |
609	
610	No new external service dependencies. MCP server runs locally via stdio.
611	
612	---
613	
614	## 7. Implementation Plan
615	
616	### File Structure
617	
618	```
619	cmd/
620	  mcp.go                    # NEW: `smoke mcp` subcommand, server bootstrap
621	  mcp_test.go               # NEW: integration test for MCP server startup
622	internal/
623	  mcp/
624	    server.go               # NEW: MCP server creation, tool registration
625	    handlers.go             # NEW: Tool handler implementations (7 tools)
626	    handlers_test.go        # NEW: Handler unit tests
627	    suggestions.go          # NEW: Fix suggestion engine
628	    suggestions_test.go     # NEW: Suggestion engine tests
629	    assertions.go           # NEW: Assertion type documentation lookup
630	    assertions_test.go      # NEW: Assertion lookup tests
631	    types.go                # NEW: Shared types (MCP result wrappers)
632	    discovery.go            # NEW: Config path resolution (walk-up logic)
633	    discovery_test.go       # NEW: Discovery tests
634	```
635	
636	### Implementation Order
637	
638	**Phase 1: Core MCP server (MVP)**
639	
640	1. `cmd/mcp.go` -- Cobra subcommand that creates and starts stdio MCP server
641	2. `internal/mcp/server.go` -- Server factory with tool registration
642	3. `internal/mcp/types.go` -- Result wrapper types
643	4. `internal/mcp/handlers.go` -- `smoke_run` handler (wraps existing `runner.Runner`)
644	5. Test: Start server, send `tools/list`, verify `smoke_run` appears
645	6. Test: Call `smoke_run` against cosmo-smoke's own `.smoke.yaml`, verify results
646	
647	**Phase 2: Read-only tools**
648	
649	7. `smoke_validate` handler -- wraps `schema.Load()` + `schema.Validate()`
650	8. `smoke_list` handler -- wraps `schema.Load()` + test enumeration
651	9. `smoke_discover` handler -- wraps `monorepo.Discover()`
652	10. `smoke_explain` handler -- static assertion docs lookup
653	11. Tests for all four
654	
655	**Phase 3: Config generation**
656	
657	12. `smoke_init` handler -- wraps `detector.Detect()` + `detector.GenerateConfig()`
658	13. `smoke_generate_test` handler -- template-based YAML generation
659	14. Tests for both
660	
661	**Phase 4: Error UX**
662	
663	15. `internal/mcp/suggestions.go` -- Fix suggestion engine for all 29 assertion types
664	16. Integrate suggestions into `smoke_run` response
665	17. Output truncation for large stdout/stderr
666	18. Tests
667	
668	**Phase 5: Long-running support**
669	
670	19. Convert `smoke_run` to task-augmented tool with `TaskSupportOptional`
671	20. Progress notifications (test N of M completed)
672	21. Test with slow-running tests (otel_trace, retry)
673	
674	**Phase 6: Polish**
675	
676	22. `internal/mcp/discovery.go` -- Config path resolution with walk-up
677	23. Claude Desktop `claude_desktop_config.json` example in README
678	24. Documentation: MCP tools reference
679	25. `go.mod` update with `mcp-go` dependency
680	
681	### Estimated Effort
682	
683	| Phase | Files | LOC (new) | Time |
684	|-------|-------|-----------|------|
685	| 1: Core server | 5 | ~300 | 2h |
686	| 2: Read-only tools | 1 | ~250 | 1.5h |
687	| 3: Config generation | 1 | ~150 | 1h |
688	| 4: Error UX | 2 | ~400 | 2h |
689	| 5: Long-running | 1 | ~100 | 1h |
690	| 6: Polish | 3 | ~150 | 1h |
691	| **Total** | **13** | **~1350** | **~8.5h** |
692	
693	### Testing Strategy
694	
695	- **Unit tests**: Each handler tested in isolation with mock `schema.Load` / `runner.Runner`
696	- **Integration test**: Start stdio MCP server, send JSON-RPC messages, verify responses
697	- **Self-smoke**: Run `smoke mcp` and call `smoke_run` against cosmo-smoke's own `.smoke.yaml`
698	- **Claude Desktop test**: Configure MCP server in Claude Desktop, run conversational smoke tests
699	
700	### MCP Prompts (Future Enhancement)
701	
702	Not in v1, but the MCP `prompts` capability could expose:
703	- `"troubleshoot_failures"` -- Claude reads recent failure output and suggests fixes
704	- `"add_test_for"` -- conversational test generation with guided follow-up questions
705	- `"migrate_from_goss"` -- walks through goss-to-smoke migration
706	
707	These would use `s.AddPrompt()` from mcp-go and reuse the `internal/migrate/goss/` package.
708	
709	---
710	
711	## Key Decisions Summary
712	
713	| Decision | Choice | Rationale |
714	|----------|--------|-----------|
715	| Binary strategy | `smoke mcp` subcommand | Single binary, simpler distribution |
716	| Transport | stdio | Claude Desktop's native MCP transport |
717	| Library | `mark3labs/mcp-go` v0.34+ | Most complete Go MCP implementation |
718	| Long-running tests | Task-augmented (`TaskSupportOptional`) | Fast tests sync, slow tests async |
719	| Error format | JSON with `fix_suggestions` array | Actionable conversational debugging |
720	| Working directory | Explicit `config_path` + walk-up | No reliance on inherited cwd |
721	| State management | Stateless tool calls | No session state, simpler, more robust |
722	| New dependency | Only `mcp-go` | Minimal dep philosophy preserved |
723

Base directory for this skill: /Users/gab/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/5.0.2/skills/executing-plans

# Executing Plans

## Overview

Load plan, review critically, execute all tasks, report when complete.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan."

**Note:** Tell your human partner that Superpowers works much better with access to subagents. The quality of its work will be significantly higher if run on a platform with subagent support (such as Claude Code or Codex). If subagents are available, use superpowers:subagent-driven-development instead of this skill.

## The Process

### Step 1: Load and Review Plan
1. Read plan file
2. Review critically - identify any questions or concerns about the plan
3. If concerns: Raise them with your human partner before starting
4. If no concerns: Create TodoWrite and proceed

### Step 2: Execute Tasks

For each task:
1. Mark as in_progress
2. Follow each step exactly (plan has bite-sized steps)
3. Run verifications as specified
4. Mark as completed

### Step 3: Complete Development

After all tasks complete and verified:
- Announce: "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
- Follow that skill to verify tests, present options, execute choice

## When to Stop and Ask for Help

**STOP executing immediately when:**
- Hit a blocker (missing dependency, test fails, instruction unclear)
- Plan has critical gaps preventing starting
- You don't understand an instruction
- Verification fails repeatedly

**Ask for clarification rather than guessing.**

## When to Revisit Earlier Steps

**Return to Review (Step 1) when:**
- Partner updates the plan based on your feedback
- Fundamental approach needs rethinking

**Don't force through blockers** - stop and ask.

## Remember
- Review plan critically first
- Follow plan steps exactly
- Don't skip verifications
- Reference skills when plan says to
- Stop when blocked, don't guess
- Never start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

content.text:
Yes, exactly. cosmo-smoke becomes an **MCP tool server**. Here's the flow:

1. User adds `"command": "smoke", "args": ["mcp"]` to their Claude Desktop config
2. Claude Desktop launches `smoke mcp` as a background process via stdio
3. Claude can now call tools like `smoke_run`, `smoke_init`, `smoke_validate`, `smoke_explain` conversationally
4. User says "run my smoke tests and tell me what's failing" — Claude calls `smoke_run`, gets structured results with fix suggestions, explains the failures in plain English

It's like giving Claude a direct API into cosmo-smoke instead of shelling out to `smoke run` via bash. The 7 tools cover the full lifecycle: discover configs, validate them, run tests, explain assertion types, generate new tests, and debug failures.

# Using Git Worktrees

## Overview

Git worktrees create isolated workspaces sharing the same repository, allowing work on multiple branches simultaneously without switching.

**Core principle:** Systematic directory selection + safety verification = reliable isolation.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace."

## Directory Selection Process

Follow this priority order:

### 1. Check Existing Directories

```bash
# Check in priority order
ls -d .worktrees 2>/dev/null     # Preferred (hidden)
ls -d worktrees 2>/dev/null      # Alternative
```

**If found:** Use that directory. If both exist, `.worktrees` wins.

### 2. Check CLAUDE.md

```bash
grep -i "worktree.*director" CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null
```

**If preference specified:** Use it without asking.

### 3. Ask User

If no directory exists and no CLAUDE.md preference:

```
No worktree directory found. Where should I create worktrees?

1. .worktrees/ (project-local, hidden)
2. ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/<project-name>/ (global location)

Which would you prefer?
```

## Safety Verification

### For Project-Local Directories (.worktrees or worktrees)

**MUST verify directory is ignored before creating worktree:**

```bash
# Check if directory is ignored (respects local, global, and system gitignore)
git check-ignore -q .worktrees 2>/dev/null || git check-ignore -q worktrees 2>/dev/null
```

**If NOT ignored:**

Per Jesse's rule "Fix broken things immediately":
1. Add appropriate line to .gitignore
2. Commit the change
3. Proceed with worktree creation

**Why critical:** Prevents accidentally committing worktree contents to repository.

### For Global Directory (~/.config/superpowers/worktrees)

No .gitignore verification needed - outside project entirely.

## Creation Steps

### 1. Detect Project Name

```bash
project=$(basename "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)")
```

### 2. Create Worktree

```bash
# Determine full path
case $LOCATION in
  .worktrees|worktrees)
    path="$LOCATION/$BRANCH_NAME"
    ;;
  ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/*)
    path="~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/$project/$BRANCH_NAME"
    ;;
esac

# Create worktree with new branch
git worktree add "$path" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
cd "$path"
```

### 3. Run Project Setup

Auto-detect and run appropriate setup:

```bash
# Node.js
if [ -f package.json ]; then npm install; fi

# Rust
if [ -f Cargo.toml ]; then cargo build; fi

# Python
if [ -f requirements.txt ]; then pip install -r requirements.txt; fi
if [ -f pyproject.toml ]; then poetry install; fi

# Go
if [ -f go.mod ]; then go mod download; fi
```

### 4. Verify Clean Baseline

Run tests to ensure worktree starts clean:

```bash
# Examples - use project-appropriate command
npm test
cargo test
pytest
go test ./...
```

**If tests fail:** Report failures, ask whether to proceed or investigate.

**If tests pass:** Report ready.

### 5. Report Location

```
Worktree ready at <full-path>
Tests passing (<N> tests, 0 failures)
Ready to implement <feature-name>
```

## Quick Reference

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| `.worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
| `worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
| Both exist | Use `.worktrees/` |
| Neither exists | Check CLAUDE.md → Ask user |
| Directory not ignored | Add to .gitignore + commit |
| Tests fail during baseline | Report failures + ask |
| No package.json/Cargo.toml | Skip dependency install |

## Common Mistakes

### Skipping ignore verification

- **Problem:** Worktree contents get tracked, pollute git status
- **Fix:** Always use `git check-ignore` before creating project-local worktree

### Assuming directory location

- **Problem:** Creates inconsistency, violates project conventions
- **Fix:** Follow priority: existing > CLAUDE.md > ask

### Proceeding with failing tests

- **Problem:** Can't distinguish new bugs from pre-existing issues
- **Fix:** Report failures, get explicit permission to proceed

### Hardcoding setup commands

- **Problem:** Breaks on projects using different tools
- **Fix:** Auto-detect from project files (package.json, etc.)

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace.

[Check .worktrees/ - exists]
[Verify ignored - git check-ignore confirms .worktrees/ is ignored]
[Create worktree: git worktree add .worktrees/auth -b feature/auth]
[Run npm install]
[Run npm test - 47 passing]

Worktree ready at /Users/jesse/myproject/.worktrees/auth
Tests passing (47 tests, 0 failures)
Ready to implement auth feature
```

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Create worktree without verifying it's ignored (project-local)
- Skip baseline test verification
- Proceed with failing tests without asking
- Assume directory location when ambiguous
- Skip CLAUDE.md check

**Always:**
- Follow directory priority: existing > CLAUDE.md > ask
- Verify directory is ignored for project-local
- Auto-detect and run project setup
- Verify clean test baseline

## Integration

**Called by:**
- **brainstorming** (Phase 4) - REQUIRED when design is approved and implementation follows
- **subagent-driven-development** - REQUIRED before executing any tasks
- **executing-plans** - REQUIRED before executing any tasks
- Any skill needing isolated workspace

**Pairs with:**
- **finishing-a-development-branch** - REQUIRED for cleanup after work complete

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/gab/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/5.0.2/skills/test-driven-development

# Test-Driven Development (TDD)

## Overview

Write the test first. Watch it fail. Write minimal code to pass.

**Core principle:** If you didn't watch the test fail, you don't know if it tests the right thing.

**Violating the letter of the rules is violating the spirit of the rules.**

## When to Use

**Always:**
- New features
- Bug fixes
- Refactoring
- Behavior changes

**Exceptions (ask your human partner):**
- Throwaway prototypes
- Generated code
- Configuration files

Thinking "skip TDD just this once"? Stop. That's rationalization.

## The Iron Law

```
NO PRODUCTION CODE WITHOUT A FAILING TEST FIRST
```

Write code before the test? Delete it. Start over.

**No exceptions:**
- Don't keep it as "reference"
- Don't "adapt" it while writing tests
- Don't look at it
- Delete means delete

Implement fresh from tests. Period.

## Red-Green-Refactor

```dot
digraph tdd_cycle {
    rankdir=LR;
    red [label="RED\nWrite failing test", shape=box, style=filled, fillcolor="#ffcccc"];
    verify_red [label="Verify fails\ncorrectly", shape=diamond];
    green [label="GREEN\nMinimal code", shape=box, style=filled, fillcolor="#ccffcc"];
    verify_green [label="Verify passes\nAll green", shape=diamond];
    refactor [label="REFACTOR\nClean up", shape=box, style=filled, fillcolor="#ccccff"];
    next [label="Next", shape=ellipse];

    red -> verify_red;
    verify_red -> green [label="yes"];
    verify_red -> red [label="wrong\nfailure"];
    green -> verify_green;
    verify_green -> refactor [label="yes"];
    verify_green -> green [label="no"];
    refactor -> verify_green [label="stay\ngreen"];
    verify_green -> next;
    next -> red;
}
```

### RED - Write Failing Test

Write one minimal test showing what should happen.

<Good>
```typescript
test('retries failed operations 3 times', async () => {
  let attempts = 0;
  const operation = () => {
    attempts++;
    if (attempts < 3) throw new Error('fail');
    return 'success';
  };

  const result = await retryOperation(operation);

  expect(result).toBe('success');
  expect(attempts).toBe(3);
});
```
Clear name, tests real behavior, one thing
</Good>

<Bad>
```typescript
test('retry works', async () => {
  const mock = jest.fn()
    .mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error())
    .mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error())
    .mockResolvedValueOnce('success');
  await retryOperation(mock);
  expect(mock).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(3);
});
```
Vague name, tests mock not code
</Bad>

**Requirements:**
- One behavior
- Clear name
- Real code (no mocks unless unavoidable)

### Verify RED - Watch It Fail

**MANDATORY. Never skip.**

```bash
npm test path/to/test.test.ts
```

Confirm:
- Test fails (not errors)
- Failure message is expected
- Fails because feature missing (not typos)

**Test passes?** You're testing existing behavior. Fix test.

**Test errors?** Fix error, re-run until it fails correctly.

### GREEN - Minimal Code

Write simplest code to pass the test.

<Good>
```typescript
async function retryOperation<T>(fn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
  for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    try {
      return await fn();
    } catch (e) {
      if (i === 2) throw e;
    }
  }
  throw new Error('unreachable');
}
```
Just enough to pass
</Good>

<Bad>
```typescript
async function retryOperation<T>(
  fn: () => Promise<T>,
  options?: {
    maxRetries?: number;
    backoff?: 'linear' | 'exponential';
    onRetry?: (attempt: number) => void;
  }
): Promise<T> {
  // YAGNI
}
```
Over-engineered
</Bad>

Don't add features, refactor other code, or "improve" beyond the test.

### Verify GREEN - Watch It Pass

**MANDATORY.**

```bash
npm test path/to/test.test.ts
```

Confirm:
- Test passes
- Other tests still pass
- Output pristine (no errors, warnings)

**Test fails?** Fix code, not test.

**Other tests fail?** Fix now.

### REFACTOR - Clean Up

After green only:
- Remove duplication
- Improve names
- Extract helpers

Keep tests green. Don't add behavior.

### Repeat

Next failing test for next feature.

## Good Tests

| Quality | Good | Bad |
|---------|------|-----|
| **Minimal** | One thing. "and" in name? Split it. | `test('validates email and domain and whitespace')` |
| **Clear** | Name describes behavior | `test('test1')` |
| **Shows intent** | Demonstrates desired API | Obscures what code should do |

## Why Order Matters

**"I'll write tests after to verify it works"**

Tests written after code pass immediately. Passing immediately proves nothing:
- Might test wrong thing
- Might test implementation, not behavior
- Might miss edge cases you forgot
- You never saw it catch the bug

Test-first forces you to see the test fail, proving it actually tests something.

**"I already manually tested all the edge cases"**

Manual testing is ad-hoc. You think you tested everything but:
- No record of what you tested
- Can't re-run when code changes
- Easy to forget cases under pressure
- "It worked when I tried it" ≠ comprehensive

Automated tests are systematic. They run the same way every time.

**"Deleting X hours of work is wasteful"**

Sunk cost fallacy. The time is already gone. Your choice now:
- Delete and rewrite with TDD (X more hours, high confidence)
- Keep it and add tests after (30 min, low confidence, likely bugs)

The "waste" is keeping code you can't trust. Working code without real tests is technical debt.

**"TDD is dogmatic, being pragmatic means adapting"**

TDD IS pragmatic:
- Finds bugs before commit (faster than debugging after)
- Prevents regressions (tests catch breaks immediately)
- Documents behavior (tests show how to use code)
- Enables refactoring (change freely, tests catch breaks)

"Pragmatic" shortcuts = debugging in production = slower.

**"Tests after achieve the same goals - it's spirit not ritual"**

No. Tests-after answer "What does this do?" Tests-first answer "What should this do?"

Tests-after are biased by your implementation. You test what you built, not what's required. You verify remembered edge cases, not discovered ones.

Tests-first force edge case discovery before implementing. Tests-after verify you remembered everything (you didn't).

30 minutes of tests after ≠ TDD. You get coverage, lose proof tests work.

## Common Rationalizations

| Excuse | Reality |
|--------|---------|
| "Too simple to test" | Simple code breaks. Test takes 30 seconds. |
| "I'll test after" | Tests passing immediately prove nothing. |
| "Tests after achieve same goals" | Tests-after = "what does this do?" Tests-first = "what should this do?" |
| "Already manually tested" | Ad-hoc ≠ systematic. No record, can't re-run. |
| "Deleting X hours is wasteful" | Sunk cost fallacy. Keeping unverified code is technical debt. |
| "Keep as reference, write tests first" | You'll adapt it. That's testing after. Delete means delete. |
| "Need to explore first" | Fine. Throw away exploration, start with TDD. |
| "Test hard = design unclear" | Listen to test. Hard to test = hard to use. |
| "TDD will slow me down" | TDD faster than debugging. Pragmatic = test-first. |
| "Manual test faster" | Manual doesn't prove edge cases. You'll re-test every change. |
| "Existing code has no tests" | You're improving it. Add tests for existing code. |

## Red Flags - STOP and Start Over

- Code before test
- Test after implementation
- Test passes immediately
- Can't explain why test failed
- Tests added "later"
- Rationalizing "just this once"
- "I already manually tested it"
- "Tests after achieve the same purpose"
- "It's about spirit not ritual"
- "Keep as reference" or "adapt existing code"
- "Already spent X hours, deleting is wasteful"
- "TDD is dogmatic, I'm being pragmatic"
- "This is different because..."

**All of these mean: Delete code. Start over with TDD.**

## Example: Bug Fix

**Bug:** Empty email accepted

**RED**
```typescript
test('rejects empty email', async () => {
  const result = await submitForm({ email: '' });
  expect(result.error).toBe('Email required');
});
```

**Verify RED**
```bash
$ npm test
FAIL: expected 'Email required', got undefined
```

**GREEN**
```typescript
function submitForm(data: FormData) {
  if (!data.email?.trim()) {
    return { error: 'Email required' };
  }
  // ...
}
```

**Verify GREEN**
```bash
$ npm test
PASS
```

**REFACTOR**
Extract validation for multiple fields if needed.

## Verification Checklist

Before marking work complete:

- [ ] Every new function/method has a test
- [ ] Watched each test fail before implementing
- [ ] Each test failed for expected reason (feature missing, not typo)
- [ ] Wrote minimal code to pass each test
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Output pristine (no errors, warnings)
- [ ] Tests use real code (mocks only if unavoidable)
- [ ] Edge cases and errors covered

Can't check all boxes? You skipped TDD. Start over.

## When Stuck

| Problem | Solution |
|---------|----------|
| Don't know how to test | Write wished-for API. Write assertion first. Ask your human partner. |
| Test too complicated | Design too complicated. Simplify interface. |
| Must mock everything | Code too coupled. Use dependency injection. |
| Test setup huge | Extract helpers. Still complex? Simplify design. |

## Debugging Integration

Bug found? Write failing test reproducing it. Follow TDD cycle. Test proves fix and prevents regression.

Never fix bugs without a test.

## Testing Anti-Patterns

When adding mocks or test utilities, read @testing-anti-patterns.md to avoid common pitfalls:
- Testing mock behavior instead of real behavior
- Adding test-only methods to production classes
- Mocking without understanding dependencies

## Final Rule

```
Production code → test exists and failed first
Otherwise → not TDD
```

No exceptions without your human partner's permission.

22		Use:   "serve",

24		Long:  "Expose a /healthz endpoint that runs smoke tests on each request",

29		servePort       string
30		servePath       string
31		serveConfigFile string
32		serveDashboard  bool

34		serveDBPath     string

37	func init() {
38		rootCmd.AddCommand(serveCmd)
39		serveCmd.Flags().StringVarP(&servePort, "port", "p", "8080", "Port to listen on")
40		serveCmd.Flags().StringVar(&servePath, "path", "/healthz", "Health endpoint path")
41		serveCmd.Flags().StringVarP(&serveConfigFile, "file", "f", ".smoke.yaml", "Config file path")
42		serveCmd.Flags().BoolVar(&serveDashboard, "dashboard", false, "Enable dashboard aggregation mode")
43		serveCmd.Flags().StringVar(&serveAPIKey, "api-key", "", "API key for POST /api/results (X-API-Key header)")
44		serveCmd.Flags().StringVar(&serveDBPath, "db-path", "smoke-dashboard.db", "SQLite database path")
45	}

1	package runner
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"context"
6		"fmt"
7		"net/http"
8		"os"
9		"os/exec"
10		"strings"
11		"sync"
12		"time"
13	
14		"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/monorepo"
15		"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/reporter"
16		"github.com/CosmoLabs-org/cosmo-smoke/internal/schema"
17	)
18	
19	// RunOptions controls test execution behavior.
20	type RunOptions struct {
21		Tags        []string
22		ExcludeTags []string
23		FailFast    bool
24		DryRun      bool
25		Timeout     time.Duration // per-test override (0 = use config)
26	}
27	
28	// SuiteResult holds the aggregate outcome.
29	type SuiteResult struct {
30		Project         string
31		Total           int
32		Passed          int
33		Failed          int
34		Skipped         int
35		AllowedFailures int
36		Duration        time.Duration
37		Tests           []TestResult
38	}
39	
40	// TestResult holds one test's outcome.
41	type TestResult struct {
42		Name           string
43		Passed         bool
44		Skipped        bool
45		AllowedFailure bool // true if test failed but allow_failure was set
46		Duration       time.Duration
47		Assertions     []AssertionResult
48		Error          error
49		Attempts       int // number of attempts made (1 = no retry)
50	}
51	
52	// Runner executes smoke tests from a config.
53	type Runner struct {
54		Config      *schema.SmokeConfig
55		Reporter    reporter.Reporter
56		ConfigDir   string
57		trace       *TraceContext
58		TraceHealth *TraceHealthTracker
59	}
60	
61	// Run executes all tests per the options and returns the suite result.
62	func (r *Runner) Run(opts RunOptions) (*SuiteResult, error) {
63		start := time.Now()
64	
65		// Run prerequisites
66		if len(r.Config.Prereqs) > 0 {
67			timeout := r.Config.Settings.Timeout.Duration
68			if timeout == 0 {
69				timeout = 30 * time.Second
70			}
71			results := CheckPrerequisites(r.Config.Prereqs, timeout)
72			for _, pr := range results {
73				r.Reporter.PrereqStart(pr.Name)
74				r.Reporter.PrereqResult(reporter.PrereqResultData{
75					Name:   pr.Name,
76					Passed: pr.Passed,
77					Output: pr.Output,
78					Hint:   pr.Hint,
79					Error:  pr.Error,
80				})
81			}
82			// Check for prereq failures
83			for _, pr := range results {
84				if !pr.Passed {
85					return nil, fmt.Errorf("prerequisite %q failed: %v", pr.Name, pr.Error)
86				}
87			}
88		}
89	
90		// Filter tests by tags
91		tests := filterTests(r.Config.Tests, opts.Tags, opts.ExcludeTags)
92	
93		// Initialize trace context if otel is enabled
94		if r.Config.OTel.Enabled {
95			r.trace = NewTraceContext()
96			if r.TraceHealth == nil {
97				r.TraceHealth = NewTraceHealthTracker(10)
98			}
99		}
100

content.subject: Phase 4: Error UX (suggestion engine + truncation)
content.description: Build fix suggestion engine for all 29 assertion types. Output truncation for large stdout/stderr. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 6: Polish (discovery walk-up, docs, config example)
content.description: Config path resolution with walk-up logic. Claude Desktop config example. Documentation. Final test suite run.

content.subject: Phase 1: Core MCP server + smoke_run handler
content.description: Create cmd/mcp.go (Cobra subcommand), internal/mcp/server.go (server factory + tool registration), internal/mcp/types.go (result wrappers), internal/mcp/handlers.go (smoke_run handler). TDD: write tests first. Add mcp-go dependency.

content.subject: Phase 2: Read-only tools (validate, list, discover, explain)
content.description: Add smoke_validate, smoke_list, smoke_discover, smoke_explain handlers. All wrap existing internal packages. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 3: Config generation tools (init, generate_test)
content.description: Add smoke_init and smoke_generate_test handlers. Wraps detector package + template generation. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 5: Long-running support (task-augmented tools)
content.description: Convert smoke_run to task-augmented tool with TaskSupportOptional. Progress notifications. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 4: Error UX (suggestion engine + truncation)
content.description: Build fix suggestion engine for all 29 assertion types. Output truncation for large stdout/stderr. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 6: Polish (discovery walk-up, docs, config example)
content.description: Config path resolution with walk-up logic. Claude Desktop config example. Documentation. Final test suite run.

content.subject: Phase 1: Core MCP server + smoke_run handler
content.description: Create cmd/mcp.go (Cobra subcommand), internal/mcp/server.go (server factory + tool registration), internal/mcp/types.go (result wrappers), internal/mcp/handlers.go (smoke_run handler). TDD: write tests first. Add mcp-go dependency.

content.subject: Phase 2: Read-only tools (validate, list, discover, explain)
content.description: Add smoke_validate, smoke_list, smoke_discover, smoke_explain handlers. All wrap existing internal packages. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 3: Config generation tools (init, generate_test)
content.description: Add smoke_init and smoke_generate_test handlers. Wraps detector package + template generation. TDD.

content.subject: Phase 5: Long-running support (task-augmented tools)
content.description: Convert smoke_run to task-augmented tool with TaskSupportOptional. Progress notifications. TDD.

content.description: Convert smoke_run to task-augmented tool with TaskSupportOptional. Progress notifications. TDD.
